content.text: The Handoff Problem in GenAI Projects

PoC teams optimise for demonstration speed. Operations teams optimise for maintainability, reliability, and cost control. When a GenAI PoC 'succeeds' and is handed to Ops without a structured knowledge transfer, the result is predictable: the system runs for 60 days without incident, then a model update silently degrades quality; no one knows how to re-evaluate because the eval dataset lived in a personal Colab notebook. Alternatively, a 3 AM alert fires about abnormal token spend, but the runbook says nothing about cost incidents because no one imagined the budget could be exhausted overnight by a single misconfigured batch job.

Operational handoff requirements formalise what the PoC team must transfer before they disband or move to the next project. The goal is not a knowledge dump — it is a structured transfer that leaves the Ops team genuinely capable of running, monitoring, updating, and debugging the system without going back to the PoC authors.

attrs.alt: Five knowledge packages required for PoC-to-Ops handoff

content.text: The Five Knowledge Packages
Every operational handoff must deliver five packages. Partial delivery of any one creates a known operational risk that must be logged and assigned an owner and deadline before the PoC team moves on.
Package 1: System Design Documentation

The Ops team must be able to understand the system without asking the PoC authors. This means more than an architecture diagram — it means documenting every external dependency, every model version pin, every prompt in the system, every known limitation, and every corner the PoC deliberately cut.
Artefact
Minimum content
Common gap in PoC handoffs
Architecture diagram
Component boxes, data flow arrows, external API calls, storage systems
Diagram only shows happy path; failure paths and fallback routes missing
Model card
Model name, version, provider, context window, known failure modes, content policy
Model version pinned in code but not documented; next provider update breaks silently
Prompt registry
Every system prompt and few-shot example in version control, with change history
Prompts live in developer notebooks or hardcoded strings; ops cannot audit them
PoC cut-corners log
Every shortcut taken during PoC that must be addressed before or after production
Shortcuts are informal and forgotten; technical debt surfaces as incidents
Data flow map
Where PII enters, is processed, is stored, and is deleted; provider data retention clauses
Inferred from code review; GDPR exposure found post-launch
Package 2: Runbook

A runbook is the single most critical handoff artefact for on-call teams. It must be written during the PoC, not after, because the PoC authors have the context that Ops will lack. A runbook is not a README — it is a procedure manual for specific operational events.

# runbook.yaml — FNOL Claim Chatbot
system: fnol-claim-chatbot
version: 1.3.0
owner: platform-ai-team
oncall: #platform-ai-oncall (Slack)
escalation:
  - L1: on-call engineer (15 min response)
  - L2: platform-ai-team lead (30 min)
  - L3: LLM provider support + CTO (P0 only)

procedures:

  health_check:
    trigger: every 5 minutes via synthetic monitor
    steps:
      - GET /health → expect 200 with {"status": "ok", "model_available": true}
      - If model_available=false: check provider status page; page on-call if >5 min
      - Check Grafana dashboard 'FNOL AI Overview' for latency P95 spike

  cost_spike:
    trigger: billing alert > $500/hour
    steps:
      - Check /metrics/token_usage — identify which tenant/user is driving volume
      - If runaway loop: call POST /admin/circuit-breaker/open with reason="cost_spike"
      - This triggers graceful-degradation mode (static FAQ only) for 30 minutes
      - Alert finance-ops channel; open incident ticket
      - Root cause: check for prompt injection or malformed session state

  model_drift:
    trigger: quality_score_p50 drops below 0.72 for 2 consecutive hours
    steps:
      - Run eval suite: make eval-run ENV=production SAMPLE=200
      - Compare scores to baseline in /evals/baselines/v1.3.0.json
      - If regression confirmed: page platform-ai-team lead
      - Do NOT rollback without platform-ai-team approval
      - Mitigation: enable shadow_mode=true to capture failing cases for analysis

  provider_outage:
    trigger: error_rate > 0.05 for 5 minutes
    steps:
      - Check provider status: https://status.openai.com
      - If confirmed outage: POST /admin/fallback-mode/enable (serves static FAQ)
      - Notify customer-success channel with ETA from provider status page
      - When provider recovers: POST /admin/fallback-mode/disable; monitor for 15 min

The runbook above illustrates the level of specificity required. Each procedure must include the trigger condition, the exact steps with commands, the decision points, the escalation path, and the expected outcome. Vague runbooks ('check the logs and restart if needed') are worse than no runbook because they create false confidence.
Package 3: Observability Specification

The PoC team must hand over a fully specified observability setup — not just a running dashboard, but the specification that lets Ops recreate, extend, and understand it. GenAI systems require metrics that conventional APM tools do not capture by default.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Optional

@dataclass
class MetricSpec:
    name: str
    description: str
    unit: str
    slo_target: Optional[str]
    alert_threshold: Optional[str]
    pii_risk: bool = False

@dataclass
class ObservabilitySpec:
    service: str
    metrics: list[MetricSpec] = field(default_factory=list)

    def pii_safe_metrics(self) -> list[MetricSpec]:
        return [m for m in self.metrics if not m.pii_risk]

    def slo_metrics(self) -> list[MetricSpec]:
        return [m for m in self.metrics if m.slo_target]

# FNOL chatbot observability specification
fnol_obs = ObservabilitySpec(
    service="fnol-claim-chatbot",
    metrics=[
        MetricSpec("request_latency_p95", "95th-percentile end-to-end latency", "ms",
                   slo_target="< 5000ms", alert_threshold="> 4500ms for 5m"),
        MetricSpec("first_token_latency_p95", "Time to first streamed token", "ms",
                   slo_target="< 800ms", alert_threshold="> 700ms for 5m"),
        MetricSpec("input_tokens_per_request_p95", "Token cost proxy", "tokens",
                   slo_target=None, alert_threshold="> 6000 (prompt injection risk)"),
        MetricSpec("quality_score_p50", "LLM-judge score on sampled requests", "score 0-1",
                   slo_target="> 0.75", alert_threshold="< 0.72 for 2h"),
        MetricSpec("hallucination_flag_rate", "% responses flagged by hallucination detector", "%",
                   slo_target="< 2%", alert_threshold="> 3% for 30m"),
        MetricSpec("cost_per_request_usd", "Blended cost including retrieval", "USD",
                   slo_target="< $0.015", alert_threshold="> $0.02 (sustained 15m)"),
        MetricSpec("error_rate", "HTTP 5xx + provider errors / total requests", "%",
                   slo_target="< 1%", alert_threshold="> 5% for 5m"),
        MetricSpec("session_transcript", "Full user + assistant transcript", "text",
                   slo_target=None, alert_threshold=None, pii_risk=True),
    ]
)

print("SLO metrics:")
for m in fnol_obs.slo_metrics():
    print(f"  {m.name}: {m.slo_target}")

print("\nPII-safe metrics (safe to export to shared dashboard):")
for m in fnol_obs.pii_safe_metrics():
    print(f"  {m.name}")
Package 4: Evaluation and Drift Management

Quality drift is the most insidious production risk in GenAI systems. A model provider update, a change in retrieval corpus, or a shift in user behaviour can all degrade output quality without triggering any conventional error rate or latency alert. The PoC team must hand over the tools and knowledge needed to detect, diagnose, and respond to drift.
Artefact
Content
Without it, Ops cannot...
Eval dataset
Curated (input, expected_output, metadata) triples; split by category; held out from fine-tuning
Measure whether a model update improved or degraded quality
Baseline score file
Scorer output for the current production model on the eval dataset
Know what score to regress against; any drift is invisible
Scorer implementation
The exact LLM-judge or rule-based scorer used to produce baseline scores
Reproduce scores consistently; judge prompts must be versioned
Re-eval trigger rules
When to re-run evals (model update, config change, 2-week cadence, quality alert)
Know whether to rerun proactively or only reactively
Drift detection code
Statistical comparison of live sample scores vs baseline; alert threshold
Detect gradual degradation; only catch hard failures
Human review queue spec
Which response types go to human review; what reviewers score; how scores feed back
Close the feedback loop; quality improvements are guesses
Package 5: Compliance and Governance Records
Compliance artefacts generated during the PoC must transfer to the team that will own them in production. They cannot live in a PoC team member's personal files or an ephemeral project space.
Data Processing Agreement (DPA) and Master Service Agreement (MSA) with the model provider — stored in the legal contract management system, not a team Drive folder.
Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) or AI Impact Assessment — signed by the DPO, filed in the compliance register.
Fairness audit results — methodology, dataset used, disparity metrics, sign-off date, and next review date.
Red-team report — prompt injection attempts, PII exfiltration tests, scope violation tests, and results.
Explainability record — what level of explanation the system provides, approved by business and legal owners.
Access control specification — who can call the API, who can read logs (with PII), who can modify prompts.
Handoff Acceptance Criteria

A handoff is not complete when the PoC team believes they have transferred knowledge — it is complete when the Ops team can demonstrate they can operate the system independently. The acceptance gate requires the Ops team to perform specific tasks without PoC team assistance.
Task
Ops team must be able to...
Incident response
Triage a synthetic latency alert and follow the runbook to resolution without asking the PoC team
Quality measurement
Run the eval suite against the production model and interpret the score against the baseline
Cost investigation
Identify which tenant or request pattern is driving a cost spike and invoke the circuit breaker
Prompt audit
Locate every system prompt in version control and explain the purpose of each section
Rollback
Execute a full rollback to the previous deployment including model version and prompt version
Compliance question
Answer a DPO question about where user data goes and how long it is retained

If the Ops team cannot pass these tasks without consulting the PoC authors, the handoff is not complete. The PoC team must remain available until all tasks are demonstrated successfully — this is the contractual completion criterion for the PoC engagement.
Handoff Anti-patterns
Anti-pattern
Consequence
Corrective practice
'It's all in the code'
Ops must reverse-engineer design decisions from implementation details; months of context lost
Require architecture docs and cut-corners log as explicit deliverables, not optional
Handoff by demo recording
Recording goes stale; Ops cannot ask follow-up questions; operational edge cases not covered
Require live shadow-on-call session where Ops handles a real alert with PoC team present
Eval dataset in Colab notebook
Dataset is inaccessible after notebook environment expires; baseline scores cannot be reproduced
Eval datasets in version-controlled repository; scorer runs in CI on every model change
Runbook written by Ops
Ops writes what they guess should happen; PoC team's actual knowledge of failure modes is lost
PoC team writes first draft; Ops reviews and runs tabletop exercise; PoC team signs off on accuracy
Compliance docs in PoC Jira
Project archive closes; legal artefacts inaccessible for audits 18 months later
All compliance artefacts filed in permanent systems before PoC project closure
No shadow on-call period
First real incident is also first time Ops handles an AI-specific failure mode alone
Require 2-week shadow period where PoC team is available for every alert; explicit sign-off to end
Handoff Checklist Template
The following checklist is designed to be completed jointly by the PoC team lead and the Ops team lead before the PoC team's final engagement date.

## PoC Operational Handoff Checklist
**System:** [system name]  **Version:** [version]  **Date:** [date]

### Package 1: System Design
- [ ] Architecture diagram reviewed and accepted by Ops team
- [ ] All external dependencies listed with SLA and support contacts
- [ ] Model card complete (name, version, provider, context limit, known failures)
- [ ] Prompt registry in version control; all prompts reviewed by Ops
- [ ] Data flow map reviewed by DPO or privacy lead
- [ ] PoC cut-corners log reviewed; each item has owner + target resolution date

### Package 2: Runbook
- [ ] Runbook covers: health check, restart, rollback, cost spike, model drift, PII incident, provider outage
- [ ] Tabletop exercise completed: Ops team walked through each procedure
- [ ] On-call rotation updated; Ops team lead is primary
- [ ] Escalation path tested: page sent to L2 and confirmed received

### Package 3: Observability
- [ ] Grafana (or equivalent) dashboard live and reviewed by Ops
- [ ] All SLO alerts enabled and routed to correct channels
- [ ] Log schema documented; PII fields identified and scrub verified
- [ ] Cost-per-request metric live; billing alert threshold set and tested

### Package 4: Evaluation and Drift
- [ ] Eval dataset in version-controlled repository (not personal storage)
- [ ] Baseline score file committed alongside eval dataset
- [ ] Scorer runs in CI; re-eval trigger documented
- [ ] Drift detection alert enabled; threshold set; Ops team can interpret results
- [ ] Human review queue process documented and queue is accessible

### Package 5: Compliance
- [ ] DPA filed in contract management system
- [ ] DPIA / AI impact assessment in compliance register
- [ ] Red-team report filed; open findings have owners
- [ ] Fairness audit results filed with next review date
- [ ] Explainability record filed and approved

### Acceptance Gate
- [ ] Ops team completed synthetic incident response without PoC team assistance
- [ ] Ops team ran eval suite and interpreted results
- [ ] Ops team demonstrated cost investigation and circuit breaker
- [ ] Shadow on-call period scheduled (start: [date]; end: [date])

**Handoff accepted by:**
PoC Team Lead: _________________ Date: _______
Ops Team Lead:  _________________ Date: _______
Shadow On-Call Period

A shadow on-call period is a structured 2-week window between handoff completion and full Ops ownership where the PoC team remains available for every alert. It is not optional — it is the mechanism that validates the handoff artefacts against real production events.
Shadow Period Structure
Week
Arrangement
Goal
Week 1
Ops team is primary responder; PoC team observes and annotates
Ops team builds muscle memory; PoC team identifies gaps in runbook
Week 2
Ops team is sole responder; PoC team on standby (< 30 min response)
Validate Ops team can operate fully independently
End of week 2
Exit interview: every incident reviewed; runbook gaps closed
Formal sign-off that handoff is complete

Every alert during the shadow period is logged with: time, nature of alert, Ops team actions taken, time to resolution, and any runbook gap discovered. Gaps discovered during shadow are treated as blocking — the shadow period extends until they are resolved and re-tested.
Prompt Registry Handoff
System prompts are business-critical configuration that must be version-controlled, auditable, and accessible to Ops without developer involvement. A prompt registry formalises this.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Optional
import hashlib, json, datetime

@dataclass
class PromptVersion:
    version: str
    content: str
    author: str
    rationale: str
    approved_by: Optional[str]
    created_at: str
    is_current: bool = False

    def content_hash(self) -> str:
        return hashlib.sha256(self.content.encode()).hexdigest()[:12]

@dataclass
class PromptEntry:
    prompt_id: str
    purpose: str
    component: str        # e.g. "triage_classifier", "claim_drafter"
    versions: list[PromptVersion] = field(default_factory=list)

    def current_version(self) -> Optional[PromptVersion]:
        return next((v for v in self.versions if v.is_current), None)

    def audit_trail(self) -> list[dict]:
        return [
            {
                "version": v.version,
                "hash": v.content_hash(),
                "author": v.author,
                "approved_by": v.approved_by,
                "created_at": v.created_at,
                "rationale": v.rationale,
            }
            for v in sorted(self.versions, key=lambda x: x.created_at)
        ]

# Example: FNOL triage system prompt registry entry
triage_prompt = PromptEntry(
    prompt_id="fnol-triage-v2",
    purpose="Classify incoming claim descriptions by peril type and urgency",
    component="triage_classifier",
    versions=[
        PromptVersion(
            version="1.0.0",
            content="You are an insurance claims triage specialist...",
            author="alice@company.com",
            rationale="Initial PoC version — no few-shot examples",
            approved_by=None,
            created_at="2024-11-01T09:00:00Z",
        ),
        PromptVersion(
            version="1.1.0",
            content="You are an insurance claims triage specialist...\n\nExamples:\n...",
            author="bob@company.com",
            rationale="Added 5 few-shot examples after accuracy dropped to 0.68 on flood claims",
            approved_by="carol@company.com",
            created_at="2024-12-15T14:30:00Z",
            is_current=True,
        ),
    ]
)

current = triage_prompt.current_version()
print(f"Current prompt: {current.version} (hash: {current.content_hash()})")
print(f"Approved by: {current.approved_by}")
print("Audit trail:")
for entry in triage_prompt.audit_trail():
    print(f"  {entry['version']} by {entry['author']}: {entry['rationale']}")

The registry must be stored in version control (not a database) so that every prompt change appears in git history alongside the code change that deployed it. Ops teams must be able to check out any past prompt version and reproduce historical behaviour — this is essential for debugging quality regressions.
Model Update Response Protocol

Model provider updates — whether announced version increments or silent capability changes — are the most common source of post-handoff quality regressions. The PoC team must hand over a documented protocol for responding to them.
Update type
Detection method
Response protocol
Announced version increment (e.g. gpt-4o → gpt-4o-2025-01)
Provider release notes; version pin in config
Run full eval suite before updating; require score >= baseline - 0.02 to proceed
Silent capability update (same version, changed behaviour)
Scheduled eval run detects drift; quality alert fires
Capture failing examples; report to provider; pin to last known good if available
Deprecation notice (version end-of-life)
Provider release notes; 6-month advance notice typical
Evaluate replacement model 12 weeks before EOL; migration plan approved by product owner
Embedding model change
Retrieval quality drops; semantic similarity scores shift
Re-embed entire corpus on new model; run retrieval quality eval before switching

Never update a model version in production without first running the eval suite and comparing to baseline. A 2% quality drop sounds small but can represent a doubling of escalation rate or complaint volume for a customer-facing system.
Knowledge Transfer Verification Matrix
Use this matrix to confirm that each knowledge domain has been transferred, not just delivered. Delivery means the artefact exists. Transfer means the Ops team can act on it without help.
Knowledge domain
Delivery check
Transfer verification task
System design
Architecture doc exists and is current
Ops team can explain the data flow for a claim submission without looking at code
Failure modes
Cut-corners log complete
Ops team can name the three most likely failure scenarios and their mitigations
Prompt management
Prompt registry in version control
Ops team can update a prompt, review diff, get approval, and deploy without PoC team
Eval pipeline
Eval dataset and scorer in CI
Ops team can trigger an eval run, read the report, and decide whether to proceed with a model update
Cost management
Budget alerts configured
Ops team can identify the top cost driver from the metrics dashboard and invoke cost controls
Compliance records
All artefacts in permanent systems
Ops team can answer 'where does user data go?' with a specific reference to the DPA
Summary

Operational handoff requirements define what the PoC team must transfer before disbanding: system design documentation, a runbook, an observability specification, evaluation and drift management tooling, and compliance records. The handoff is complete only when the Ops team can independently handle a synthetic incident, run the eval suite, investigate a cost anomaly, and answer a compliance question — all without asking the PoC authors.

Writing handoff requirements before the PoC begins — not as a post-PoC activity — forces PoC teams to build systems that are transferable, not just demonstrable. Every PoC shortcut documented in the cut-corners log is a production risk that now has a named owner and deadline rather than becoming an undocumented assumption that surfaces as an incident.
